preamble: The shift from the alveolar fricative [s] to the voiced alveolar stop [d] is a form of fortition (hardening). While rare as a standalone adult sound law, it is a hallmark of child language development and serves as a phonetic parallel to the widespread 'stopping' of dental fricatives in many English and Germanic dialects.

examples.note: Developmental stopping: substituting a voiced or voiceless stop for a fricative.
While starting from a dental fricative, this represents the broader systemic trend of fricative-to-stop hardening in the alveolar region.
